evidence_quotes.quote: hidden gambling mechanics in games, especially on mobile phone games.
No "loot boxes" or anything that yields a random item (or collection of items) - this is basically gambling with extra steps.
Many games start with a simple digital market where one buys digital tokens and then using those tokens they buy items directly.
other programs that are slowly moving towards a subscription model
a game that becomes unusable after a while is essentially a scam.

paragraphs.text: First, to tackle addiction and gambling problems in society I think we need to outright ban any advertising of gambling. That means on TV, on billboards (this is probably not legal already in most places), and on the internet. Banning a specific kind of ad on the internet is probably a more difficult thing to enforce but having written in law would at least start things moving in the right direction. Second, there's a lot of hidden gambling mechanics in games, especially on mobile phone games. These phone games mostly target children which increases the chance of them becoming gambling addicts as adults as well. We need to completely ban any sort of "random" (i.e. gambling) mechanic when purchasing digital items. It will be a hard sell to game companies since they make a lot of money out of it but it's a necessity to ensure our children are protected. Many games start with a simple digital market where one buys digital tokens and then using those tokens they buy items directly. I think this should be the only way to buy digital items - a clear amount of digital tokens for a specific thing. No "loot boxes" or anything that yields a random item (or collection of items) - this is basically gambling with extra steps. Third, in order to ensure we don't lose valuable art and online communities, we need to also tackle the fact that many games and other online services are paywalled and can be destroyed on a whim by the companies that create them. Especially for games that have no reason to be connecting to a central server, the fact that one can "buy" a game that becomes unusable after a while is essentially a scam. Once you own a game and have it on your computer or phone it should just continue working for as long as you want. It is the same with computer programs such Adobe Photoshop, Microsoft Office and other programs that are slowly moving towards a subscription model, while the technical improvements added year after year yield diminishing returns for the price one pays. Editing photos or documents for the average person has been solved for at least 10-20 years. We do not need further improvements and what we need is programs that just work. It would be difficult to force Microsoft or Adobe to change their financial tactics but there are open source alternatives so maybe what this act should do is also advertise for these free and open source options. This is especially important in governmental institutions - they should by default not force the citizens to use a proprietary program to deal with institutions. It is producing an unnecessary cost on everyone and hurts the least affluent the most. I have more ideas but this is getting long already, hoping you'll read it and take it into account. A passionate IT professional,
